metadata.delivery_architecture: Pure-template delivery: 13 domains × ~4 canonical answers × 3 wrappers (neutral / warmer / pushback-y). Wrapper selected DETERMINISTICALLY from user-input surface features (skepticism markers > hesitation markers > neutral default). No LLM in response loop.

A1.1.title: Cash vs in-kind — default
A1.1.canonical_answer: Generally, cash is the most useful kind of donation in the first days after a disaster. Money gives the response team flexibility to buy what's actually needed as conditions on the ground change, and it converts into help much faster than physical items shipped from far away. In-kind donations matter when a recognized responder has published a specific, current list of needed items and there's a coordinated way to receive them. Without that, items can end up sitting in warehouses or being discarded. For most donors, sending money to a verified organization is the highest-impact choice.
A1.1.source_basis: USAID 'Cash is Best' (Medium); IFRC CVA (50% by 2025 commitment); IFRC unsolicited-donations principle; USAGov; Logistics Cluster UBD guidelines (2015)
A1.1.author_notes: Default modality answer. Anchors in cross-institutional consensus. No specific cost number here — saved for A1.3.

A1.2.title: Cash vs in-kind — when in-kind makes sense
A1.2.canonical_answer: There are cases when sending physical items is the right call — but they're narrower than most people think. The conditions are usually three together: a recognized responder has published a current needs list, the specific item is on that list, and there's a coordinated way for the item to reach them. Without all three, items often end up sitting in warehouses, getting damaged in transit, or being discarded later. If those conditions aren't in place, money to the same organization tends to do more good — and you can ask the organization what they're using it for.
A1.2.source_basis: Logistics Cluster UBD guidelines (2015); Sphere Handbook 2018; Kızılay 17.2.2023 needs-list precedent (Turkish-context example, not cited in canonical to keep country-neutral); Pacheco-2022 in-kind quality field experiment
A1.2.author_notes: Three-conditions rule is the load-bearing teaching. Note cross-cite to D6 (earmarking) in last sentence.

A1.3.title: Cash vs in-kind — why is cash better operationally
A1.3.canonical_answer: The main reasons are speed, cost, and flexibility. Shipping physical goods internationally is far more expensive than buying the same things closer to where they're needed — USAID has shown, for example, that sending 100,000 liters of bottled water overseas costs around $350,000, while an in-country purification system delivering the same volume costs about $300. Cash also converts faster, because the response team avoids customs, sorting, and transport delays. And it stays useful as the situation changes — needs in the first 48 hours look different from needs in week two, and money can shift between them in a way that pre-shipped items can't.
A1.3.source_basis: USAID 'Cash is Best' (Medium) — primary-source verified for the $350k/$300 water ratio; Harvey-2007 (HPG); IFRC CVA; Bao-2025; Rafiei-2022
A1.3.author_notes: Uses the USAID water cost ratio with proper attribution ('USAID has shown', 'for example', 'around'). 1-2 day phase reference in 'first 48 hours' framing.

A1.4.title: Cash vs in-kind — but I want to send something tangible
A1.4.canonical_answer: That impulse is common and not unreasonable — sending a physical thing feels more direct than sending money. The trade-off is that the response team has to receive, sort, transport, and distribute whatever arrives, and that work takes resources away from the actual relief operation. If keeping a sense of choice over your donation matters to you, most reputable organizations let you direct cash toward specific kinds of need — like 'shelter,' 'food,' or 'medical care' — without the logistics burden of physical items. The end result is usually that your money reaches the response faster and stays useful longer.
A1.4.source_basis: Penta-2019 (Weberian donor sociology); Zagefka-2015 (donor psychology); Daniels-2021 (donor aversion to rejection); IFRC unsolicited-donations 'generosity gone awry'
A1.4.author_notes: Acknowledges the impulse without dismissing it (per Zagefka). Cross-cites D6 earmarking as the middle-ground option.

A2.1.title: Item fit — what makes an item useful?
A2.1.canonical_answer: Whether an item is useful depends on several things together: whether the response team has assessed it as currently needed, whether it fits the climate and the disaster context, whether it meets specifications (size, type, condition), and whether there's a coordinated way to get it to where it's needed. An item that's helpful in one disaster can be useless or even counterproductive in another. The reliable way to check is to look at what recognized responders have publicly listed as currently needed — if it's not on that list right now, sending it generally doesn't help much.
A2.1.source_basis: Sphere Handbook 2018; IFRC Unsolicited Donations; Pacheco-2022; Kızılay 17.2.2023 needs-list precedent
A2.1.author_notes: Foundational item-fit framing. The 4-conditions rule (need + climate + spec + coordination) is the load-bearing teaching.

A2.2.title: Item fit — used vs new items
A2.2.canonical_answer: Used items create their own problem: they have to be sorted, cleaned, sized, and checked for condition before they can be distributed, and that work needs people and infrastructure that disaster zones usually don't have spare. After major recent disasters, official needs lists have specifically asked for new items only — used clothing in particular has been refused outright in many responses, because the sorting burden ends up diverting volunteers from higher-priority tasks. If you have unused items in good condition that match what's currently being requested, they're more likely to actually reach someone.
A2.2.source_basis: USAID 'Cash is Best' (38 countries ban used-clothing imports); AFAD second-hand-goods ban precedent (Türkiye 2023, country-neutral framing); IFRC unsolicited donations
A2.2.author_notes: AFAD ban referenced generically ('major recent disasters') to stay country-neutral. Closes with constructive option (unused + on-list).

A2.3.title: Item fit — why isn't [specific item] on the menu?
A2.3.canonical_answer: What gets surfaced on a donation platform usually reflects what active responders have specifically requested at this stage of the response. Items that aren't shown may not be needed right now (the local supply is adequate), may be too logistically complex to receive in bulk, or may require qualified channels that individual donors can't easily access. If there's something specific you want to send, a cash donation to a recognized responder is often the better path — they can buy it locally if it's actually useful, and it doesn't add to the sorting and transport burden.
A2.3.source_basis: Logistics Cluster UBD guidelines; PSAid; FEMA donations mgmt

A2.4.title: Item fit — climate and season fit
A2.4.canonical_answer: Climate is one of the most consistent ways items go wrong. Winter coats sent to tropical climates, cooling gear sent to freezing zones, and heavy clothing sent in summer have all been documented as common mismatches. The same principle applies to the timeline: an item that fits the immediate weather might be obsolete a few weeks later as the season shifts. For the first 1-2 days of a response specifically, items that match the current local climate and immediate survival needs (food, water, warmth in winter, shade and hydration in heat) are the most likely to fit.
A2.4.source_basis: USAID 'Cash is Best' (climate-mismatch examples); MSF Türkiye/Syria (winter context); Sphere Handbook (climate/context standards)
A2.4.author_notes: Climate-match principle. 1-2 day phase scope explicit.

A3.1.title: Acute timing — what's needed in the first 1-2 days?
A3.1.canonical_answer: In the first 1-2 days after a disaster, the priorities are typically immediate survival: search and rescue, trauma care, safe drinking water, ready-to-eat food, and shelter from the elements (especially in extreme weather). Specific needs depend on the disaster type and the local conditions, but money tends to be the most useful donation in this window because the response team can buy exactly what's needed as the situation clarifies. Pre-positioned partner agencies usually have stocks ready to deploy; what they often need most is the cash flexibility to scale up rapidly.
A3.1.source_basis: IFRC Pakistan urgent appeal (acute phase); MSF Türkiye/Syria response; Sphere Handbook; OCHA situation reports
A3.1.author_notes: Anchors the 1-2 day scope. Names concrete acute priorities.

A3.2.title: Acute timing — how quickly does my donation convert to help?
A3.2.canonical_answer: Cash donations to verified responders typically convert into help on the ground within 48 hours, because the response team can use that money to buy or release pre-positioned supplies right away. Physical items shipped from far away usually take days or weeks, depending on customs, transport, and sorting capacity at the receiving end. If you donate today, money goes to work faster than a box on a truck. That's especially true in the first 1-2 day window when response logistics are still ramping up.
A3.2.source_basis: USAID 'Cash is Best' (speed argument); IFRC CVA; Harvey-2007
A3.2.author_notes: 48-hour conversion claim is concrete and survives source review. Cross-cites D1 implicitly.

A3.3.title: Acute timing — will my donation matter later too?
A3.3.canonical_answer: Both, generally — but in different ways. In the first 1-2 days, donations support immediate survival operations. As the response continues, the same donated funds shift toward longer-term needs: shelter rebuilding, water and sanitation systems, livelihood support, and eventually disaster preparedness for the next event. Cash donations are especially good at this transition because they don't have an expiry date — the organization can spend them as priorities evolve. Donations made today often end up funding work that continues for months or even years.
A3.3.source_basis: IFRC Pakistan rebuilding (phase-2 evidence); MSF 6 months NW Syria; Six months of humanitarian action; OCHA pooled funds

A3.4.title: Acute timing — how do needs change over time?
A3.4.canonical_answer: The shape of disaster response shifts across phases. The first 1-2 days are search-and-rescue and immediate survival. Week 1 typically focuses on shelter, hot meals, and trauma care. Weeks 2-4 transition into water and sanitation systems, mental health support, and cash assistance for affected families. Months 1+ move into housing reconstruction, livelihood restoration, and infrastructure repair. Cash donations stay useful across all these phases because they can be redirected as priorities change — that flexibility is hard to match with pre-shipped items.
A3.4.source_basis: OCHA situation reports; IFRC Pakistan year-1 vs year-2; MSF 6 months NW Syria; Six months of humanitarian action

A4.1.title: Logistics — what's the 'second disaster'?
A4.1.canonical_answer: The 'second disaster' is the term researchers use for the operational chaos that unsolicited donations can create after a disaster. When uncoordinated goods arrive in large volumes — clothing, food, medical supplies, water — the response team has to spend time and resources sorting, storing, transporting, and disposing of items that often aren't usable. Estimates from past disasters suggest that 60% or more of spontaneously arriving cargo is non-priority, and a smaller fraction is high-priority survival supplies. The result is supply chains clogged with the wrong stuff while critical needs go underserved.

A4.2.title: Logistics — what happens to donated items after they arrive?
A4.2.canonical_answer: Donated physical items follow a pipeline: they arrive at a port or warehouse, get unloaded, sorted by type and condition, stored until needed, transported to distribution points, and finally given out. Each step requires staff and infrastructure. Items that don't match current needs sit in storage, sometimes for months. Damaged, expired, or culturally inappropriate items get discarded — and disposal itself can be expensive and environmentally costly. After Hurricane Dorian in 2019, one country reportedly spent over half a million dollars just disposing of unrequested goods. That money could have funded recovery work directly.

A4.3.title: Logistics — why aren't more items accepted in disaster responses?
A4.3.canonical_answer: Acceptance isn't really about generosity — it's about whether the receiving end has the capacity to use what's offered. Items have to fit current needs, arrive in usable condition, clear customs and inspection, and reach the right distribution point at the right time. If any of those break down, the item becomes a burden rather than help. Responders often publish narrow item lists precisely because they've learned the hard way that 'anything helpful' tends to generate more cleanup than help. The cash channel exists partly to absorb donor generosity without that operational cost.

A4.4.title: Logistics — has this actually caused problems in past disasters?
A4.4.canonical_answer: It's well documented. After the 2010 Haiti earthquake, trucks of essential supplies were delayed up to five days at the Dominican Republic border because Port-au-Prince warehouses were saturated with unsolicited cargo. After Hurricane Katrina, a 'non-stop caravan of 18-wheelers' occupied warehouse space needed for high-priority emergency supplies. After Hurricane Dorian (Bahamas, 2019), 70 tonnes of unrequested kitchen sets, 1,008 food parcels, and 5,000 mattresses arrived — much of it spoiled, requiring expensive disposal. The pattern is consistent enough that researchers and humanitarian agencies have named it the 'second disaster.'

A5.1.title: Trust — how do I know my donation reaches survivors?
A5.1.canonical_answer: The simplest checks are: is the organization registered as a charity in a country with oversight, does it publish annual reports or audited financial statements, and has it been operating with field presence in disaster response over time? Verified organizations typically show where funds went — how much went to direct aid, what went to logistics and overhead, and what's still being deployed. Some publish per-disaster transparency pages with specific spending breakdowns. Donations through unverified channels — social media solicitations, copycat URLs, requests for gift cards or cryptocurrency — are exactly where most disaster-related fraud happens.

A5.2.title: Trust — how is the organization audited?
A5.2.canonical_answer: Most major humanitarian organizations operate under formal audit requirements — either from national regulators (the UK Charity Commission, the US IRS Form 990 disclosures, state attorneys general), from international transparency frameworks (the International Aid Transparency Initiative, IATI), or from independent rating services (Charity Navigator, GuideStar). After Hurricane Sandy, for example, the New York Attorney General tracked $575 million in charitable giving to ensure funds reached intended uses. That kind of post-disaster audit is now relatively standard for major disasters. The audit trail isn't perfect, but it's far more substantive than 'trust us.'

A5.3.title: Trust — what's the overhead / admin cost?
A5.3.canonical_answer: Most reputable humanitarian organizations spend something between 5% and 25% on administration, fundraising, and infrastructure, with the rest going to programs. That 'overhead' isn't waste — it covers staff, logistics, audits, and the systems that make the response actually work. An organization with a 5% overhead might be running on volunteer labor and short-cutting safety procedures; one with 25% might be funding the surge logistics that get aid moving in 48 hours. The more useful question is whether the organization is transparent about where the overhead goes, not just how big the number is.

A5.4.title: Trust — how do I check if an organization is legitimate?
A5.4.canonical_answer: A few practical checks: confirm the organization is registered as a charity in a country that requires it, look for an annual report or audited financial statements on their official website (not a third-party page), verify the URL matches the organization's actual domain (scammers often clone names with minor variations), and check whether they're listed on independent watchdog or rating sites. Legitimate organizations don't usually pressure for urgency on payment method choice — if you're being pushed toward gift cards, cryptocurrency, or wire transfers, that's a strong scam signal.

A6.1.title: Earmarking — can I direct my money to specific needs?
A6.1.canonical_answer: Most large humanitarian organizations let donors restrict their contribution to a specific purpose — a particular country, a particular type of program (shelter, food, medical), or a particular disaster. This is called 'earmarking.' It can feel reassuring to know your money will go where you want it. The trade-off is that earmarking limits the organization's ability to redirect funds to wherever the need turns out to be highest. For most donors, lightly directed donations (e.g., 'this disaster response') hit a balance: you stay connected to a specific event, but the org keeps room to adapt.

A6.2.title: Earmarking — what's the problem with strict earmarking?
A6.2.canonical_answer: Tightly earmarked funding can create real operational headaches. If donors restrict money to a specific high-profile disaster, the organization may end up overfunded for that event while underfunded for less-visible crises happening at the same time. Money tied to 'shelter' can't easily move to 'medical care' if the on-the-ground priority shifts mid-response. Save the Children has reported that around 91% of its humanitarian funding comes in as earmarked — that proportion makes it harder to fund the global infrastructure that even the earmarked response depends on.

A6.3.title: Earmarking — what is flexible / unrestricted funding?
A6.3.canonical_answer: Unrestricted funding is money the organization can use wherever the assessed need is greatest. It funds 'forgotten' crises that don't get media attention, covers the operational backbone (transport, staff, IT, security) that all responses depend on, and gives the organization the flexibility to shift money as priorities evolve. Major humanitarian agencies and donor governments have committed under the 'Grand Bargain' to increase the share of flexible funding because earmarking has structural costs. For individual donors, choosing the organization's general emergency fund (rather than a specific named disaster) is the equivalent of giving them that flexibility.

A6.4.title: Earmarking — what's the trade-off for the organization?
A6.4.canonical_answer: Earmarking and unrestricted funding represent a structural tension. Earmarking drives more total donations (donors give more when they feel they're directing the spend), but each earmarked dollar can do less because it can't be redirected as needs change. Unrestricted funding gives the organization maximum operational efficiency per dollar, but it's harder to fundraise for because the messaging is less concrete. Most large agencies use a mix: highly earmarked campaigns for visible events drive volume, while internal pooled funds provide the flexibility to deploy where the need is actual highest.

A7.1.title: Local procurement — is buying locally better than shipping aid in?
A7.1.canonical_answer: Generally yes, if local markets are still functioning. Cash given to a response team that buys locally tends to be faster, cheaper, and more dignified than items shipped from overseas — and it puts money into the local economy at exactly the moment it's most needed. The IFRC has committed to delivering 50% of its humanitarian assistance through cash or vouchers by 2025 partly for this reason. The main exception is when markets have been destroyed or access is blocked, in which case external supplies have to come in. In the first 1-2 days specifically, immediate survival needs usually dominate over economic-recovery considerations.

A7.2.title: Local procurement — what is Cash and Voucher Assistance?
A7.2.canonical_answer: Cash and Voucher Assistance is humanitarian help delivered as money — either physical cash, bank transfer, mobile money, or vouchers redeemable at local shops — rather than as physical goods. The household receiving it decides what to buy, based on their own situation. CVA tends to be faster, more efficient, and more dignified than traditional in-kind aid, because it respects the recipient's own priorities. The main organizations operating CVA at scale include the IFRC, the World Food Programme, and the UN, plus most major national Red Cross and Red Crescent societies. CVA is now a standard humanitarian modality, not an experimental one.

A7.3.title: Local procurement — could cash cause local inflation?
A7.3.canonical_answer: It can, in principle — flooding a market with cash without enough goods available creates price spikes. In practice, humanitarian organizations doing CVA at scale also run market analyses to set transfer values that match local prices and prevent destabilization. The Disasters Emergency Committee, for example, reportedly adjusted cash transfer values four times over an 18-month response to keep purchasing power stable as inflation moved. The risk is real but well-known; it doesn't make cash worse than items in most contexts, but it does mean cash works best when delivered through organizations that monitor the local market.
A7.3.source_basis: DEC reports (referenced from deep-research doc 2 — primary not in /QAsources); IFRC CVA

A7.4.title: Local procurement — how does cash support local recovery?
A7.4.canonical_answer: Cash spent in local markets does double work: it meets the immediate need (food, hygiene, household items) AND it injects revenue into local businesses at the moment they're trying to reopen. Shopkeepers, suppliers, transporters, and producers all benefit, which speeds the broader economic recovery. The opposite — large volumes of imported aid — can undercut local merchants and delay their recovery by months or years. In the first 1-2 days after a disaster, the survival argument for cash dominates; in later weeks, the local-economy argument becomes the bigger reason.
A7.4.source_basis: IFRC CVA; Cash Hub: Our approach; USAID 'Cash is Best'

A8.1.title: Safety/medical — can I send medicine to disaster victims?
A8.1.canonical_answer: As an individual donor, no — sending medicine to a disaster zone is one of the few near-absolute don'ts in humanitarian giving. The World Health Organization has explicit guidelines: pharmaceutical donations should only move through qualified channels (national health ministries, recognized medical NGOs, formal procurement systems) and only when the affected country or response coordinator has specifically requested them. Personal-donor medicine shipments routinely arrive expired, mislabeled in the wrong language, in unfamiliar formulations, or completely inappropriate for the local disease profile. They're typically intercepted and destroyed by customs.
A8.1.source_basis: WHO Medicine Donations Guidelines (2010); FDA pharmaceutical donation guidance; PQMD framework

A8.2.title: Safety/medical — what about my unused or leftover medications?
A8.2.canonical_answer: Unused household medications should not be sent to disaster zones, even if they're still in date and look fine. WHO guidelines state that medicines unsuitable for use in the donor country are unsuitable for use anywhere — the same standards apply. Free samples, partially used bottles, or medicines returned to pharmacies for disposal all fall into this category. For getting rid of unused medication safely, most pharmacies in donor countries have take-back programs. If you want to help with medical needs after a disaster, the most effective path is cash to organizations that procure medicines through qualified channels.

A8.3.title: Safety/medical — what harm can inappropriate medical donations cause?
A8.3.canonical_answer: The historical record is uncomfortable. After the Bosnia conflicts, an estimated 30,500 tons of medicine were donated against a need of roughly 1,800 tons — and 50 to 60 percent of what arrived was inappropriate for the population's actual disease profile. Disposal of pharmaceutical waste requires specialized high-temperature incineration and can cost between two and five US dollars per kilogram. A separate World Bank project had to spend around four million dollars just disposing of inappropriate pharmaceutical donations stockpiled in Croatia. Inappropriate medicine donations effectively transfer disposal costs to the affected country.

A9.1.title: Local vs intl NGO — should I donate to local or international?
A9.1.canonical_answer: The most defensible answer is: pick whatever is verified, active, coordinated, and has field presence where the disaster is unfolding. Local and international actors bring different strengths. Local organizations — like the Turkish Red Crescent (Kızılay) in Türkiye, Caritas national chapters, or other established civil-society responders — usually have on-the-ground access, cultural familiarity, and existing community trust. International organizations bring scale, surge logistics, and access to global fundraising. In most major disasters, both are needed and both are working together. The choice is rarely 'local or international' so much as 'is this specific organization credible and coordinated.'

A9.2.title: Local vs intl NGO — what advantages do local responders have?
A9.2.canonical_answer: Local responders are usually already in the affected region when the disaster happens, which means they can act in the first hours rather than the first days. They speak the language, understand the cultural context, and know the local infrastructure (which routes are passable, which neighborhoods are most vulnerable, which community leaders to coordinate with). They're often more trusted by the affected population than international organizations arriving from outside. In Türkiye, for example, the Turkish Red Crescent (Kızılay) coordinated with civil society and government during the 2023 earthquake response in ways no external organization could have done as quickly.

A9.3.title: Local vs intl NGO — what do international NGOs add?
A9.3.canonical_answer: International organizations bring three things local actors usually can't match at scale: surge logistics (airlifting personnel and supplies into disaster zones), global fundraising reach (mobilizing donations from countries far from the disaster), and technical expertise developed across many response contexts. They also bring accountability frameworks — most are subject to international audit and reporting standards. The trade-off is that international organizations typically take longer to mobilize than already-present locals, may have less cultural familiarity, and depend on local partners for actual ground operations. In practice, most major responses use both: international for scale and surge, local for access and delivery.

A9.4.title: Local vs intl NGO — why are both types needed?
A9.4.canonical_answer: Disasters at scale tend to overwhelm any single response capacity. The first 1-2 days depend heavily on whoever was already present — typically local responders, national emergency-management agencies, and civil society. As the response scales over the following weeks, international organizations bring resources that local actors don't have: surge personnel, specialized equipment, supply chains that span multiple countries, and the fundraising capacity to keep the operation funded for months. The two layers are complementary, not competing. A donor who gives to either is supporting part of the same coordinated response.

A12.1.title: Anti-fraud — how do I avoid donation scams?
A12.1.canonical_answer: Disaster-related scams are common — fraudulent fundraisers spike in the days and weeks after major events, exploiting donors' urgency and emotion. The most reliable defenses are: donate only to organizations you've verified independently (not just ones that appeared in a social media post or unsolicited email), check that the organization is registered as a charity in a country with oversight, and use the organization's official website directly rather than following links from emails or texts. Federal Trade Commission and FBI guidance both flag the same patterns: pressure to give immediately, requests for unusual payment methods, and copycat names or URLs.
A12.1.source_basis: FTC Turkey/Syria alert; CalOES; FBI scam warnings (deep-research doc); FEMA

A12.2.title: Anti-fraud — what payment methods should I avoid?
A12.2.canonical_answer: Legitimate humanitarian organizations accept standard payment methods — credit/debit cards, bank transfers, established online donation platforms. They do not request donations via gift cards, cryptocurrency, wire transfers to personal accounts, or peer-to-peer payment apps to individual recipients. Any solicitation pushing those methods is a scam signal that's reliable enough to act on. The same applies to anyone claiming to be a federal employee (FEMA, IRS, etc.) requesting payment to 'expedite' relief — government agencies do not solicit donations or fees from individuals during disaster response.

A12.3.title: Anti-fraud — how do I verify an organization is legitimate?
A12.3.canonical_answer: A few practical checks: confirm the organization is registered as a charity in a country with regulatory oversight, look up the org on an independent watchdog or rating site (Charity Navigator, GuideStar, your country's equivalent), check that the website URL matches the organization's actual official domain (scammers often create cloned sites with slight variations), and look for an annual report or audited financial statements published on the official site. Major established organizations like national Red Cross / Red Crescent societies, IFRC, UN agencies, MSF, and the well-known Catholic / Protestant / Muslim humanitarian networks are generally safe defaults if you're unsure.

A12.4.title: Anti-fraud — what if I think I've been scammed?
A12.4.canonical_answer: If you suspect you've been scammed, the practical steps are: contact your bank or card issuer to dispute the charge (timing matters — sooner is much better), report the scam to your country's consumer-protection authority (the FTC in the US, your national consumer agency elsewhere), and report the fake site to the platform that hosted the link if applicable. After major recent disasters, regulators have actively pursued scammers and recovered funds in some cases. Reporting matters even if you can't recover your money — it helps the agency disrupt the operation for other potential victims.

A13.1.title: Religious/cultural channels — generic overview
A13.1.canonical_answer: In most countries with established religious institutions, faith-based humanitarian organizations operate alongside secular ones — and many have decades of disaster-response experience plus local networks that secular organizations lack. Catholic charities (Caritas, Catholic Relief Services), Protestant networks (Tearfund, World Vision, IOCC), Islamic relief organizations (Islamic Relief, BAZNAS in Indonesia, TDV in Türkiye), Jewish federations, and Sikh networks (Khalsa Aid) all run legitimate operational humanitarian work. The same verification rules apply: is the organization registered, audited, and active on the ground? Religious affiliation doesn't substitute for those checks, but it doesn't disqualify either.

A13.2.title: Religious channels — Türkiye
A13.2.canonical_answer: In Türkiye, the main religious-affiliated donation channels include the Türkiye Diyanet Vakfı (TDV) and Diyanet İşleri Başkanlığı for Muslim donors, and Caritas Türkiye for Catholic donors. The Turkish Red Crescent (Kızılay) operates as the main secular comparator. All three operate under formal regulation and publish operational reports.

A13.3.title: Religious channels — India

sections.content:
You are the Donation Coordinator on the ReliefBridge platform, helping users decide how to allocate their donation budget for an active disaster response.

ROLE
- Provide concrete, useful information about lead times, local needs, accountability, modality choices, item details, and off-menu item handling.
- You DO NOT advocate, persuade, or push donors toward any modality. Inform; let them decide.
- For questions about yourself (the AI), disclose honestly: pre-vetted library, GPT-4 backend, response team operates and maintains you.

TONE
- Substantive and direct. Skip sycophancy ("Great question!", "I understand your concern").
- No emoji or casual chatter in answers.
- No "I'm just an AI, but..." disclaimers.
- Speak with institutional voice, not personal voice.

KNOWLEDGE BOUNDARIES
- You know only what local responders, partner agencies, and platform operators have reported for THIS response.
- For off-menu items, explain WHY they're not in the menu (institutional procurement, regulatory, logistical).
- You do NOT speculate. If you don't know, say so and redirect.

ANSWER STYLE
- 35-60 words per answer. Hard cap 70.
- Lead with the factual answer. Include one concrete number where applicable.
- For modality/trust questions, include regret-avoidance reassurance.

CONVERSATION LIMITS
- Maximum 6 user turns per session. Cap enforced server-side, NOT by you.
- Out-of-scope: refuse + redirect to topic chip.

Six trust-trait design choices (Arms 2 & 3; only chrome differs):

1. CONTROLLABILITY — User types own questions; semantic routing matches to pre-vetted answer. Can close chat anytime; turn budget visible.
2. TRANSPARENCY — Arm 3: persistent header attribution to joint TCAs (IFRC + RC/RC + ER + Health + Fire). Answers cite audit-trail handles.
3. INTELLIGENCE — Tailored answers parameterized by region, disaster phase, item availability. Concrete numbers, not hedged generalities.
4. ADAPTABILITY — Semantic routing matches user question to closest pre-vetted answer. 3-level routing: cluster → sub-cluster → specific Q&A. Bridges for medium-confidence; refusals for out-of-scope.
5. EMPATHY (substantive, NOT sycophantic) — Regret-avoidance framing in Cluster 1 & 3 answers. NO 'I understand' preambles.
6. SECURITY / INTEGRITY — Arm 3 chrome cites joint TCAs. Institutional voice, not personal voice.

COMMON AI PITFALLS TO AVOID (Arms 2 & 3):

✕ No avatar with a face
✕ No personal name ('Sarah', 'Aria', 'Max')
✕ No sycophantic preambles ('Great question!', 'I love that you're asking…')
✕ No 'I'm just an AI, but...' disclaimers
✕ No emoji or casual tone in answers
✕ No anthropomorphic gestures ('*nods*', 'happy to help!')
✕ No upselling or persuading toward specific modality
✕ No speculative or hedged language when factual answer available
✕ No mid-answer attribution to sources (attribution lives in header chrome only)
✕ No length-padding ('To answer your question more thoroughly...')

EXCEPTION: AI-meta Q&As (Q3.M1-Q3.M3) DO discuss the AI's own nature, but in institutional voice and only when explicitly asked.
